title: Segmented Fields
text: Segmented Fields Some databases support special storage formats, such as BLOBs, for fields that can be very long. This storage format allows the record to contain a pointer or reference to the data instead of the data itself. The field data can be retrieved or stored in multiple segments using special functions available in the API. Uniface uses the generic term segmented field for this storage format. Uniface is always capable of handling segmented fields. If the DBMS does not support segmented fields, Uniface automatically maps the segmented field at the model level to the variable part of the record. In this case, the connector need not be aware that segmented fields are being used. When a segmented field is defined in the application model and the DBMS supports segmented fields, Uniface creates two fields on the internal level: The first field in the fixed part of the record contains the pointer or reference to the data. The name of this field is ‘S_’ followed by the name of the segmented field at the model level. The description of the second field in the variable part of the record describes the data itself. By definition, the record does not contain the data itself. For the insert and update requests, the DBMS connector is expected to fill the segment field pointer field in the control block iobuf at the position indicated by the field list structure. This field is then returned to the DBMS connector on subsequent fetch requests so that the DBMS connector can fetch the segmented field. The DBMS connector must also set the length of the field in the field list structure. If the DBMS connector does not require a pointer field to manipulate segmented fields, the DBMS connector should set the pointer field length to zero in the field list structure before returning from the fetch request. Note: The Uniface kernel also uses the value of the segmented field pointer. When the Uniface kernel is checking to see if a record has been changed by another user since its initial retrieval, it is the values of the segmented field pointers that are compared and not the actual values of the segmented fields themselves. For DBMS connectors that do not require a segmented field pointer, this can result in changes in the segmented field data not being detected. The second field is present in the field list only to describe the data. The second field is typically used by the connector to determine whether the segmented field is a String field or a Binary field. The name of the second field equals the name of the segmented field as defined in the application model. It is the responsibility of the DBMS connector to handle the segmented field during the Insert, Fetch, Update and Delete requests. In these requests, the segments of the segmented fields are passed directly between the Uniface kernel and the DBMS connector using the service functions, ugetblob and uputblob and buffers allocated by the connector. If more than one segmented field is present in the record, the field or fields should be handled in the order that they appear in the field list structures. During the Insert, Update and Fetch requests, the DBMS connector examines the contents of the uctrl control block member vsfnr . If it is not zero, there are segmented fields present which the DBMS connector must handle. If vsfnr is zero, this does not mean that there are no segmented fields defined for the entity, but rather that they are not required during the current request. The Uniface kernel does this for requests such as the fetch lock (mode 1) and fetch position (mode 2) requests where the segmented field is not required. If vsfnr is zero, the service functions ugetblob and uputblob do not transfer any data. The field list structure representing the segmented field itself specifies if the segmented field contains raw or binary data. If the udbfmt member of the structure is equal to the value 2, the segmented field may contain raw data. Related Links Segmented Fields: String Packing Codes (SC and SU) Segmented fields provide an alternative storage method for large amounts of data that would be impractical to store in a normal database column. Large data objects can include audio- and video clips, or objects that functionally belong to other applications but are under the control of Uniface. Such objects are referred to as Binary Large Objects (BLOBs). ugetblob Get segmented data from the Uniface kernel. uputblob Used during Fetch to send the segmented data to the Uniface kernel. uctrl Exchanges general information between Uniface and the connector, and describes a table.
